name: Sarno, Dawn M.
recent_1: Sarno, D. M., & Allan, J. N. (2025). Untangling the Web of Deceit: Examining Shared User Susceptibility Across Five Types of Digital Deceptions. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251363406
recent_1_synopsis: Building on prior work showing shared vulnerability across phishing, fake news, and scam texts, this study extends the analysis to five distinct types of digital deception to test whether a common set of users is susceptible across all of them and whether universal psychological predictors exist. It uses a behavioral classification paradigm paired with individual-difference measures to identify cross-domain susceptibility profiles. The work advances a unifying account of deception susceptibility rather than treating each scam type in isolation.
recent_2: Sabo, K. E., Sarno, D. M., & Black, J. T. (2026). Beyond the inbox: Exploring shared cognitive mechanisms underlying susceptibility to and reporting of deception across digital domains. Applied Ergonomics, 135, 104771. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104771
recent_2_synopsis: This paper investigates the cognitive mechanisms shared across different digital deception domains, examining not only susceptibility but also the under-studied behavior of reporting deception. It links individual cognitive and decision-making factors to both falling for and flagging deceptive content. The findings inform the design of training and reporting interventions that generalize beyond email phishing.
recent_3: Sarno, D. M., Gendron, C., & Volante, W. G. (2024). Filtering Out the User?: Are Users Complacent with Phishing Emails Due to Automated Filters? International Journal of Human-Computer Interaction, 41(16), 9908-9917. DOI: 10.1080/10447318.2024.2429759
recent_3_synopsis: This study tests whether the presence of automated spam/phishing filters induces complacency that degrades users' own vigilance toward phishing emails. Through controlled email-classification experiments manipulating perceived filter presence, it examines how automation reliance shifts human detection behavior. The results have practical implications for how security automation is presented to end users without undermining human oversight.
seminal_1: Sarno, D. M., Lewis, J. E., Bohil, C. J., & Neider, M. B. (2019). Which Phish Is on the Hook? Phishing Vulnerability for Older Versus Younger Adults. Human Factors: The Journal of the Human Factors and Ergonomics Society, 62(5), 704-717. DOI: 10.1177/0018720819855570
seminal_1_synopsis: Approximately 48 citations (Crossref, 2026-06-17). This is one of Sarno's foundational and most-cited works, establishing age-related differences in phishing vulnerability and showing that older and younger adults differ in susceptibility and in the cues they rely on. It is frequently cited because it brought a rigorous human-factors and aging-cognition lens to phishing detection, anchoring much of her subsequent program on individual differences in deception susceptibility.
seminal_2: Sarno, D. M., Harris, M. W., & Black, J. (2023). Which phish is captured in the net? Understanding phishing susceptibility and individual differences. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 37(4), 789-803. DOI: 10.1002/acp.4075
seminal_2_synopsis: Approximately 29 citations (Crossref, 2026-06-17). This paper is foundational for reconciling conflicting findings about the psychological predictors of phishing susceptibility, using a diverse online sample and a broad email set with measures of phishing awareness, age, impulsivity, curiosity, and the Big-5 personality traits. It is influential because it provides a comprehensive individual-differences map of who is vulnerable and why, widely cited in cybersecurity-cognition research.
expertise_overview: Dawn M. Sarno studies the cognitive and human-factors underpinnings of susceptibility to digital deception, with a primary focus on phishing emails and, more broadly, scam text messages, fake news, and other forms of online manipulation. Her work emphasizes individual differences (age, impulsivity, curiosity, personality, working memory, phishing awareness) as predictors of who falls for deception and explores shared cognitive mechanisms across deception types. Methodologically she relies on controlled email/message classification experiments, behavioral susceptibility paradigms, individual-difference surveys, and intervention/training studies. A second strand of her research concerns applied visual cognition, visual working memory, attention, and executive function, including depth perception and computer-aided detection in medical imaging (e.g., BI-RADS, mammography). She also develops and evaluates anti-phishing training interventions, examining persistence of training effects and complacency induced by automated security filters. Her domains span cybersecurity, aging, human-automation interaction, and applied cognitive psychology.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Disambiguated via the four pre-verified Human Factors anchor papers, all of which resolved on Crossref with matching titles. Identity corroborated by Clemson University AVANT Lab faculty/lab page (dmsarno.people.clemson.edu), ResearchGate (Dawn-Sarno, Assistant Professor, Clemson Department of Psychology), and UCF Psychology profile (PhD in Human Factors/Cognitive Psychology, UCF, 2020; now Assistant Professor at Clemson). Note Crossref lists co-author Mark B. Neider on the 2021 'So Many Phish' anchor and Jeffrey Black on the 2023 'Who Gets Caught' anchor. Crossref author search returns many unrelated 'Sarno' records (Indonesian agricultural/education journals) that were excluded; only cognitive-psychology/human-factors/cybersecurity records co-authored by Dawn M. Sarno were used. All cited DOIs verified to resolve via Crossref API on 2026-06-17: 10.1177/00187208251363406, 10.1016/j.apergo.2026.104771, 10.1080/10447318.2024.2429759, 10.1177/0018720819855570, 10.1002/acp.4075. Citation counts are from Crossref is-referenced-by-count on 2026-06-17 (seminal_1 = 48, seminal_2 = 29) and are typically lower than Google Scholar counts. No unverifiable citations. Volume/issue/pages for the 2025 HF and 2026 Applied Ergonomics articles taken from Crossref (Applied Ergonomics vol 135, art. 104771); the 2025 HF article is online-first without final volume/issue at time of verification.
